title: Humidity
content: Low relative humidity removes moisture from vegetation, creating fuel that ignites easily. Humidity below 30% significantly increases wildfire probability. Each 10% drop in humidity approximately doubles fire spread rate.

title: Temperature
content: High temperatures dry out vegetation and soil. Temperatures above 35°C create extreme fire danger. Sustained heat waves compound risk by reducing fuel moisture over consecutive days.

title: Wind Speed
content: Wind provides oxygen to fires and carries embers ahead of the flame front. Wind speeds above 15 m/s can cause explosive fire growth. Wind direction determines spread pattern.

title: Vapor Pressure Deficit
content: Vapor Pressure Deficit (VPD) measures atmospheric drying power. VPD above 3 kPa indicates severe atmospheric demand for moisture from vegetation. VPD is a stronger predictor of fire activity than temperature or humidity alone.

title: Vegetation Health
content: Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI) measures vegetation health and density from satellite imagery. Low EVI values indicate stressed or sparse vegetation. Areas with declining EVI are more susceptible to fire.

title: Wildfire Risk Levels
content: Wildfire risk is classified into five levels: Low (0-20%), Moderate (20-40%), High (40-65%), Very High (65-85%), and Extreme (85-100%). Risk is calculated using machine learning models trained on historical fire data, weather patterns, and vegetation conditions.

title: Fire Spread Mechanism
content: Wildfire spreads through three mechanisms: surface fire (burns ground-level vegetation), crown fire (spreads through tree canopies), and spotting (wind-carried embers ignite new fires ahead). Spread rate depends on wind speed, slope, and fuel moisture.

title: Wildfire Prevention
content: Key prevention measures include: maintaining firebreaks around settlements, clearing dry vegetation within 30 meters of structures, avoiding open burning during high-risk periods, reporting smoke immediately, and following local fire restrictions.

title: Jim Corbett National Park
content: Jim Corbett National Park in Uttarakhand spans 520 km² of Himalayan foothills. It contains sal forests, grasslands, and riverine habitats. The park experiences seasonal fires during pre-monsoon months (March-June) when dry conditions prevail.

title: Kanha Tiger Reserve
content: Kanha Tiger Reserve in Madhya Pradesh covers 940 km² of central Indian sal and mixed deciduous forests. Fire season typically runs from February to May. The reserve uses controlled burning as a management tool to reduce fuel loads.

title: Periyar Tiger Reserve
content: Periyar Tiger Reserve in Kerala spans 925 km² in the Western Ghats. Its tropical evergreen and semi-evergreen forests have high moisture content year-round, resulting in lower natural fire frequency compared to dry deciduous forests.

title: Similipal Biosphere Reserve
content: Similipal Biosphere Reserve in Odisha covers 2,750 km² of eastern Indian moist deciduous and dry deciduous forests. It forms part of the Eastern Ghats and experiences moderate fire activity during the dry season from January to April.

title: Kaziranga National Park
content: Kaziranga National Park in Assam spans 430 km² of Brahmaputra floodplain. Its tall grasslands and wetlands burn periodically as a natural ecological process. Managed burns help maintain grassland habitat for rhinos and other wildlife.

title: Emergency Response
content: During a wildfire: call emergency services immediately. Move away from the fire perpendicular to wind direction. Seek shelter in cleared areas or water bodies. Do not attempt to outrun a fire uphill. Close all windows and doors if sheltering indoors.

title: Prediction Model
content: The wildfire prediction model uses XGBoost trained on 21 features including weather data (temp, humidity, wind, VPD), vegetation indices (EVI), land cover types, elevation, and seasonal patterns. The model outputs a probability score between 0-100% for any location in India.

title: SHAP Explanations
content: SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values explain which factors contributed most to each prediction. Positive SHAP values increase fire risk, negative values decrease it. Key features typically include VPD, temperature, humidity, and vegetation indices.

title: Seasonal Patterns
content: India's fire season peaks during March-May (pre-monsoon) when vegetation is driest. The monsoon (June-September) suppresses fire activity. Post-monsoon (October-November) and winter (December-February) see moderate fire activity, especially in southern and central regions.

title: Climate Change Impact
content: Climate change is extending fire seasons globally. Rising temperatures and altered precipitation patterns increase the frequency and intensity of extreme fire weather. India has observed a lengthening of its fire season by 2-3 weeks over recent decades.

title: Land Cover Types
content: Different land cover types have varying fire susceptibility: dry deciduous forests and grasslands are most fire-prone, tropical evergreen forests are least prone, and cropland fires are often agricultural burns. The model uses 11 land cover categories as prediction features.

title: FAQ - How to use dashboard
content: To use the dashboard: click any point on the map while Predict mode is active to see wildfire risk. Use the layers panel to toggle heatmap visibility. Select protected reserves from the left panel to zoom to specific areas. Toggle Active Fires to see real-time NASA FIRMS detections.
